chamomile.desc: Sun-blessed and gentle. Heals the body, quiets the restless mind. The Celts drank it at dawn to greet the light.
chamomile.descFull: Beloved of the sun and the hearth, chamomile heals the body and stills the restless mind. Ancient Celts honored it as a solar herb, drunk at dawn to greet the light. Sacred to Brigid; gathered on Imbolc for the year's hearth-fires.
chamomile.historicUses: Druidic dawn-rite tea. Strewn on Beltane fires. Pressed into salves for sun-burned skin and into pillows for restless children.
chamomile.compounds: Apigenin, bisabolol, chamazulene; flavonoids. Mild sedative via GABA-A binding. Anti-inflammatory. Antispasmodic. Caffeine-free.
chamomile.cautions: Ragweed (Asteraceae) allergy cross-reaction possible. Avoid therapeutic doses in pregnancy. May potentiate warfarin and CNS depressants.

lavender.desc: Sacred to moon and Brighid. Soothes the spirit, opens the heart, blesses unions, invites peaceful sleep.
lavender.descFull: Sacred to the moon and Brighid, lavender soothes the spirit and opens the heart to love. Burned in ceremony to bless unions and invite peaceful slumber. Strewn beneath thresholds to deflect bitter words. The herb of soft endings.
lavender.historicUses: Roman bathhouse strewing herb. Medieval love-drafts. Burned at Imbolc for marriage blessings. Pressed under pillows for prophetic dreams.
lavender.compounds: Linalool, linalyl acetate; flavonoids, coumarins. Anxiolytic and sedative in aroma and oral use. Mild antiseptic. Calms the autonomic nervous system.
lavender.cautions: Topical sensitization possible with repeated exposure. May potentiate sedatives. Not for undiluted oral use in children under 12.

rosemary.desc: Herb of memory and watch. Druids burned it to sanctify ground and sharpen the mind before ritual.
rosemary.descFull: Herb of remembrance and vigilance. Druids burned rosemary to sanctify sacred ground and sharpen the mind before ritual. Bound to the bride's wrist to ensure faithful memory. Hung in doorways to seal the threshold against forgetting.
rosemary.historicUses: Druidic ritual purifier. Roman scholar's bath. Medieval anti-plague herb. Bound to brides; laid with the dead.
rosemary.compounds: Carnosic acid, rosmarinic acid, camphor, 1,8-cineole; flavonoids. Stimulates circulation and memory. Antioxidant. Mild antimicrobial.
rosemary.cautions: Avoid therapeutic doses in pregnancy (emmenagogue). May reduce seizure threshold in high amounts. Can raise blood pressure.

sage.desc: The wise-woman herb. Walks between worlds. Celtic seers burned it to open pathways of vision and knowing.
sage.descFull: The wise-woman herb, sage walks between worlds. Celtic seers burned it in ceremony to open pathways of vision and sacred knowing. Carried by midwives; laid on graves to ease the soul's passage. Old as the language itself.
sage.historicUses: Druidic seer's smoke. Midwife's offering at birth. Strewn on graves at Samhain. Wisdom-tea for the elder council.
sage.compounds: Thujone, camphor, rosmarinic acid, alpha-humulene; flavonoids. Antimicrobial. Memory-supporting via cholinesterase inhibition. Astringent.
sage.cautions: High-dose thujone is neurotoxic; culinary amounts are safe. Avoid in epilepsy. Not for extended use in pregnancy (emmenagogue).

basil.desc: Herb of love and fortune. Guards the hearth, draws prosperity. Placed at thresholds to ward off ill-spirits.
basil.descFull: Herb of love and fortune, basil guards the hearth and draws prosperity. Ancient Celts placed it at thresholds to ward off ill-spirits; planted in window boxes to bless the home. Pressed gift to a newcomer brings welcome and protection.
basil.historicUses: Threshold-guardian planting. Bridal bouquet herb. Carried in coin-purses for fortune. Steeped in oil for blessing-anointings.
basil.compounds: Eugenol, linalool, estragole, rosmarinic acid; flavonoids. Antibacterial. Digestive antispasmodic. Mild anti-inflammatory via COX inhibition.
basil.cautions: High-estragole cultivars may have hepatotoxic potential at very high intake. Culinary use considered safe. Patch-test essential oil before topical use.

mugwort.props: Divination · Dreaming · Astral Travel · Moon Magic
mugwort.desc: Sacred to moon and seer. Druids slept on mugwort pillows to walk between worlds in dream and prophecy.
mugwort.descFull: Sacred to the moon and to seers, mugwort draws dreams from the depths. Druids slept on mugwort pillows to walk between worlds in dream and prophecy. Burned at Midsummer; bundled at Samhain. The herb of the threshold.
mugwort.historicUses: Dream-pillow stuffing. Midsummer bonfire herb. Ritual smoke before scrying. Carried by travelers to prevent fatigue.
mugwort.pairings: St Johns Wort · Sage · Honey · Wormwood
mugwort.compounds: Thujone, artemisinins, sesquiterpene lactones, flavonoids, volatile oils. Bitter digestive stimulant. Antispasmodic. Mild emmenagogue.
mugwort.cautions: Contraindicated in pregnancy (potent emmenagogue). Ragweed allergy cross-reaction. Thujone accumulates with extended use; limit duration.

lily flower.desc: Blooms at the threshold of worlds. Sacred to moon and the dead reborn. Laid at graves to ease the crossing.
lily flower.descFull: The lily blooms at the threshold of worlds, sacred to moon goddesses and to the dead reborn. Ancient Celts laid lilies at graves to ease the crossing of souls. White petals strewn for purity; bound to the bride's veil for fidelity.
lily flower.historicUses: Funerary offering. Bridal veil-flower. Strewn on temple steps. Pressed for anointing oils.
lily flower.compounds: Mucilage, alkaloids (varies by species). Soothing to skin. Volatile floral aromatics. Traditional cosmetic and ritual use.
lily flower.cautions: Not for internal use. Many Lilium species are highly toxic; confirm species identity before any use. Acutely nephrotoxic to cats.

calendula flower.desc: The golden wheel of the sun. Heals wounds of flesh and spirit. Strewn on altars at dawn for the returning light.
calendula flower.descFull: The golden wheel of the sun, calendula heals wounds of flesh and spirit alike. Sacred to solar rites, strewn on altars at dawn to honor the returning light. Pressed into salves for cuts and burns; bound to ailing wounds with linen.
calendula flower.historicUses: Solar altar strewing. Wound-healing salve. Dyer's herb for sunlit cloth. Pressed into Beltane garlands.
calendula flower.compounds: Carotenoids, flavonoids (quercetin, isorhamnetin), triterpenoids, polysaccharides. Anti-inflammatory. Wound-healing. Lymphatic tonic.
calendula flower.cautions: Asteraceae allergy cross-reaction possible. Avoid in pregnancy (traditional emmenagogue). May slow blood clotting with anticoagulant drugs.

beeswax.desc: Gold of the hive. Sacred medium of light and binding. Priestesses sealed ritual vessels with it to keep power within.
beeswax.descFull: Gold of the hive, beeswax is the sacred medium of light and binding. Celtic priestesses sealed their ritual vessels with wax to preserve power within. Pressed into candles for vigil; pressed into salves to hold healing close to the skin.
beeswax.historicUses: Ritual seal for sacred vessels. Vigil candle medium. Salve binder. Writing-tablet surface. Anointing oil thickener.
beeswax.compounds: Long-chain esters (myricyl palmitate), free fatty acids, hydrocarbons. Skin emollient. Antimicrobial. Occlusive barrier. Not comedogenic.
beeswax.cautions: Topical use only in cosmetic applications. Bee-product allergy possible. Not intended for internal use in refined cosmetic preparations.

cocoa butter.desc: Food of the gods. Softens flesh, opens the heart. Carried from distant shores to nourish body and soul.
cocoa butter.descFull: Food of the gods, cacao butter softens flesh and opens the heart's sacred chamber. Carried from distant shores to apothecary halls, it nourishes body and soul. Pressed into salves for dry skin; bound into chocolates for ritual.
cocoa butter.historicUses: Mesoamerican ritual chocolate. European apothecary skin-softener. Pressed into lip-balms for winter. Stored for monthly bleeding salves.
cocoa butter.compounds: Stearic, oleic, and palmitic fatty acids; trace theobromine. Skin emollient and protectant. Slow-oxidizing; long shelf life.
cocoa butter.cautions: Topical use. Theobromine sensitivity: patch-test. Rich in saturated fat; limit oral supplementation. May clog pores in acne-prone skin.

yarrow.props: Courage · Healing · Psychic Shield · Battle Magic
yarrow.desc: Staunches wounds of body and spirit. Warriors bound it to shields. Healers closed the deep wounds of war with it.
yarrow.descFull: Yarrow staunches wounds of body and spirit. Celtic warriors bound it to shields; healers used it to close the deep wounds of war. Sacred to Achilles and to Brigid both. The herb of unbroken courage.
yarrow.historicUses: Battlefield wound-binder. Yarrow-stalk divination (I Ching). Strewn on Beltane fires. Hung in barns to protect cattle.
yarrow.compounds: Achilleine, azulenes, salicylic acid, flavonoids, sesquiterpene lactones. Hemostatic. Anti-inflammatory. Diaphoretic. Febrifuge.
yarrow.cautions: Asteraceae allergy cross-reaction. Avoid in pregnancy (emmenagogue). May potentiate anticoagulants. Photosensitization possible in some individuals.

elderflower.props: Faery Magic · Blessing · Transformation · Elder Magic
elderflower.desc: The Eldermother guards the old ways. No branch cut without her blessing. Her flowers bring faery gifts.
elderflower.descFull: The Eldermother guards the old ways. No branch may be cut without her blessing. Her flowers bring faery gifts and protect the home; her berries deepen the blood. Approach with respect; she remembers slights for generations.
elderflower.historicUses: Faery-ward planting. Midsummer-cordial flowers. Branches buried with the dead. Wand-wood for hedgewitches.
elderflower.compounds: Rutin, quercetin, flavonoids, vitamin C, triterpenes, volatile esters. Diaphoretic. Immune-supporting. Anti-catarrhal. Traditional cold and flu remedy.
elderflower.cautions: Use flowers or ripe berries only. Raw bark, leaves, and unripe berries contain cyanogenic glycosides. Confirm botanical identity before use.

peppermint.desc: Cool and swift. Clears the mind, opens the airways. Travelers carried it for protection on long roads.
peppermint.descFull: Cool and swift, peppermint clears the mind and opens the airways. Celtic travelers carried it for protection on long roads; it sharpens the senses and dispels the heaviness of sleep. The wakeful herb.
peppermint.historicUses: Traveler's pocket-herb. Funeral garland (cooling fragrance). Strewn on temple floors. Steeped for headache.
peppermint.compounds: Menthol, menthone, menthyl acetate; flavonoids, phenolic acids. Digestive antispasmodic. Decongestant. Cooling via TRPM8 receptor activation.
peppermint.cautions: Avoid in GERD (relaxes lower esophageal sphincter). Do not apply menthol near infants' faces. May interact with cyclosporine and felodipine.

valerian.props: Sleep · Calm · Dream Work · Threshold Magic
valerian.desc: Draws down the veil between waking and dream. The Celts gathered it at dusk for safe passage into Otherworld sleep.
valerian.descFull: The great Valerian draws down the veil between waking and dream. The Celts gathered it at dusk to ease passage into the Otherworld of sleep. Its root, pungent and earthy, holds the deep stillness of moss-covered stone.
valerian.historicUses: Insomnia-tea since Antiquity. Roman 'phu' for nerves. Medieval sleep-philtre. Dried root burned to calm cats and humans alike.
valerian.compounds: Valerenic acid, valepotriates, isovaleric acid, flavonoids, glutamine. Mild sedative via GABA modulation. Anxiolytic. Improves sleep latency.
valerian.cautions: May cause morning grogginess or vivid dreams. Avoid with alcohol or sedatives. Not established as safe in pregnancy or children under 12.

ginger.props: Fire · Courage · Abundance · Speed of Action
ginger.desc: Carries the fire of distant lands in its roots. Burned for speed; warms the cold and weary traveler.
ginger.descFull: Ginger carries the fire of distant lands in its roots. Burned for speed, it accelerates all workings and warms the cold and weary traveler. Pressed into cordials at Midwinter to call back the sun's heat.
ginger.historicUses: Spice-route trade good. Sailor's anti-nausea root. Medieval gingerbread for cold months. Ritual fire-warmer.
ginger.compounds: Gingerol, shogaol, zingerone, paradol; volatile oils. Anti-nausea via 5-HT3 antagonism. Anti-inflammatory (COX inhibitor). Carminative.
ginger.cautions: May potentiate anticoagulants (warfarin, aspirin) at high doses. Avoid large amounts in gallstone disease. May cause heartburn in sensitive individuals.

frankincense.props: Sacred Space · Spiritual Vision · Sun · Purification
frankincense.desc: King of resins. Prized by Druids as solar offering. Its smoke opens the gate to the divine and purifies all.
frankincense.descFull: King of resins, frankincense was prized by Druidic priests as a solar offering. Its smoke opens the gateway to the divine, purifying temple and spirit alike. Burned at the high holy days; pressed into anointing oils for kings.
frankincense.historicUses: Temple incense (Druidic, Egyptian, Christian). Royal anointing oil. Embalming resin. Burned for purification before any rite.
frankincense.compounds: Boswellic acids (AKBA), alpha-pinene, beta-boswellic acid, incensole acetate. Inhibits 5-LOX (anti-inflammatory). Traditional joint and mood support.
frankincense.cautions: May potentiate anticoagulants. Avoid during pregnancy in therapeutic doses. High oral resin doses may cause GI discomfort.

rose.props: Love · Beauty · Heart Magic · Ancestral Wisdom
rose.desc: Queen of flowers. Guards her gifts behind thorns. Lovers exchanged her as pledge; white roses honor the dead.
rose.descFull: Queen of flowers, the rose guards her gifts behind thorns. Celtic lovers exchanged roses as pledges; white roses honored the beloved dead at Samhain. Petals pressed into oil hold love's memory longer than any vow.
rose.historicUses: Lover's pledge. Funerary offering. Petal-strewing on the bride's path. Bath-water perfumer. Pressed into syrups and salves.
rose.compounds: Vitamin C (hips), polyphenols, geraniol, citronellol, quercetin, gallic acid. Mild astringent. Cardioprotective antioxidants. Anti-inflammatory.
rose.cautions: Rose hips are very high in vitamin C; large amounts may cause GI upset and increase kidney stone risk in susceptible individuals.

nettle.desc: Fierce guardian of the hedgerow. Stings those without respect. Planted at boundaries to ward off the unwelcome.
nettle.descFull: The fierce guardian of the hedgerow, nettle stings those who approach without respect. Hedgewitches planted it at boundaries to ward off all unwelcome forces. Its sting heals; its sting protects. Spring's first bitter green.
nettle.historicUses: Hedgerow boundary planting. Spring-tonic bitter green. Fiber for sailcloth and rope. Beaten on aching joints (urtication).
nettle.compounds: Iron, vitamin K, calcium, silica, chlorophyll, quercetin, lectins, beta-sitosterol. Mineral-dense. Anti-histamine. Diuretic. Anti-inflammatory.
nettle.cautions: Vitamin K content may antagonize warfarin. Diuretic effect; monitor fluid and electrolyte balance. Root extracts have different hormonal activity.

st johns wort.props: Solar Healing · Protection · Clarity · Midsummer Magic
st johns wort.desc: Sun-herb of Midsummer. Blazes with protective fire. Hung at Litha to guard against dark spirits and cleanse.
st johns wort.descFull: The sun-herb of Midsummer blazes with protective fire. Celtic healers hung it at Litha to guard against dark spirits and cleanse the spirit. Its yellow petals bleed red oil, the sun's own blood pressed into the dark.
st johns wort.historicUses: Litha (Midsummer) protective herb. Hung over doorways against ill-wishing. Pressed into red oil for wounds and melancholy.
st johns wort.compounds: Hypericin, pseudohypericin, hyperforin, flavonoids, xanthones. Serotonin, dopamine, and noradrenaline reuptake inhibition. Traditional antidepressant.
st johns wort.cautions: Potent CYP3A4 inducer; reduces efficacy of oral contraceptives, antiretrovirals, warfarin, and many others. Photosensitization. Avoid with SSRIs (serotonin syndrome risk).

dandelion.desc: Indomitable sun-child. Pushes through stone and asphalt. Blow the seeds to carry wishes to the wind-spirits.
dandelion.descFull: Indomitable sun-child, dandelion pushes through stone and asphalt alike. Blow the seeds to carry wishes to the wind-spirits and purify the blood. Every part of her serves: leaf, flower, root, seed. The herb of stubborn hope.
dandelion.historicUses: Spring wish-magic seed-blowing. Bitter-green tonic. Roasted root coffee substitute. Wine and mead flavoring.
dandelion.compounds: Vitamins A, C, K, potassium, calcium, inulin, taraxacin, flavonoids. Cholagogue. Diuretic. Bitter digestive. Antioxidant.
dandelion.cautions: Asteraceae allergy possible. Significant diuretic; adjust electrolyte intake. Contraindicated in bile duct obstruction. May potentiate diuretic drugs.

black pepper.desc: The warrior spice. Fires the blood and wards off malice. Celtic traders paid in pepper as they would in gold.
black pepper.descFull: The warrior spice of ancient trade routes, black pepper ignites the blood and drives off malice. Celtic traders valued it as gold; Roman soldiers carried it for endurance. Burns through stagnation and clears the path ahead.
black pepper.historicUses: Roman soldier ration. Medieval plague ward. Traded along Silk Road. Modern: digestive stimulant; anti-inflammatory in clinical use.
black pepper.compounds: Piperine (bioavailability enhancer), beta-caryophyllene. Digestive stimulant. Antioxidant.
black pepper.cautions: May irritate GI tract in excess.

star anise.props: Vision · Luck · Psychic Work · Moon Magic
star anise.desc: The star-shaped dreamer. Opens the inner eye and invites good fortune. Burned before divination.
star anise.descFull: Shaped like a celestial star and steeped in moon magic, star anise opens the inner eye and turns fortune toward the seeker. Burned before divination rites; placed beneath pillows to invite prophetic dreams and clarity.
star anise.historicUses: Chinese ritual incense and digestive. Medieval European love charm. Modern: anethole source; carminative; influenza antiviral precursor.
star anise.compounds: Trans-anethole, shikimic acid (oseltamivir precursor). Antifungal. Carminative.
star anise.cautions: Avoid in infants; toxic in excess.

allspice.desc: Carries the warmth of clove, pepper, and cinnamon in one. A healer of cold and a drawer of abundance.
allspice.descFull: Allspice carries the warmth of clove, pepper, and cinnamon in a single berry. Used in ceremony to draw prosperity and ease cold, stiff joints. Burned as incense to lift the spirit and invite good fortune into the hall.
allspice.historicUses: Maya ritual incense and embalming spice. Caribbean folk medicine for colds. Modern: analgesic topical; digestive tonic; food preservative.
allspice.compounds: Eugenol (analgesic, antimicrobial), quercetin, tannins. Carminative. Anti-inflammatory.

caraway.desc: Oldest cultivated spice of Europe. Guards possessions, binds the faithful heart, wards wandering spirits.
caraway.descFull: One of the oldest cultivated spices in Europe, caraway seeds were found in Stone Age settlements. Guards possessions against theft, binds the faithful heart in love, and wards off wandering spirits. Baked into bread to keep loved ones close.
caraway.historicUses: Neolithic food crop. Medieval love-binding and anti-theft charm. German digestive liqueur. Modern: IBS treatment; carminative; expectorant.
caraway.compounds: Carvone, limonene, flavonoids. Reduces IBS bloating. Antimicrobial.
caraway.cautions: May interact with anticoagulants at high doses.

dill.desc: Norse hearth herb. Hung above cradles for luck, burned against envy, steeped for dreaming in peace.
dill.descFull: A Norse hearth herb hung above cradles to grant luck and protection. Burned to clear jealousy from the hall and steeped in evening tea for peaceful dreaming. Carried by warriors before battle to steel the mind.
dill.historicUses: Norse protective charm. Egyptian medicinal herb 3000 BCE. Medieval anti-witchcraft hanging. Modern: infantile colic relief; carminative; antispasmodic.

vanilla.props: Love · Comfort · Mental Power · Lunar Grace
vanilla.desc: The orchid of sweetness. Soothes sorrow, awakens sensuality, and calls the gentle moon close.
vanilla.descFull: Born of a climbing orchid, vanilla is the herb of lunar comfort and tender love. Used in folk magic to draw affection and ease the troubled mind. The scent alone stills anxiety and coaxes the heart toward warmth and ease.
vanilla.historicUses: Aztec ritual cacao flavoring. European aphrodisiac tincture 17th c. Modern aromatherapy for anxiety. Antioxidant study ongoing.

sumac.desc: The tangy scarlet bark of the Mediterranean ridge. Tart as iron, bright as war-fire. Clears the blood.
sumac.descFull: Sumac grows on sun-scorched Mediterranean ridges, its berries ground to a ruby powder tart as iron. A protective herb used to purify and sharpen sight. Historically steeped as a cooling febrifuge and ground into dyes and medicine alike.
sumac.historicUses: Roman souring agent for sauces. Arabic medicine for fever and digestive disease. Modern: antioxidant-rich; blood sugar modulation; antimicrobial.
sumac.compounds: Gallic acid, quercetin, anthocyanins. Potent antioxidant. Anti-inflammatory.
sumac.cautions: May lower blood glucose; caution with diabetic meds.

milk thistle.desc: Guardian of the liver and spirit alike. The spined healer of Celtic roadsides. Restores what excess has worn.
milk thistle.descFull: Crowned with purple and armed with spines, milk thistle has guarded healers since antiquity. It restores the liver after harm and shields the body from toxin. The Celts found it at crossroads, a liminal plant of recovery and renewal.
milk thistle.historicUses: Ancient Greek liver tonic. Hildegard of Bingen remedy for bile. Modern: silymarin for hepatoprotection; cirrhosis adjunct; mushroom poisoning antidote.
milk thistle.cautions: Hepatoprotective. May lower blood sugar. Rare Asteraceae allergy.

skullcap.props: Calm · Spirit Work · Dream Gate · Peace
skullcap.desc: The dreamer's helmet. Stills the thundering mind and holds open the door between waking and vision.
skullcap.descFull: Named for its helmet-shaped flowers, skullcap stills the thundering mind and eases frayed nerves. Celtic and Native traditions alike used it as a gateway herb, holding the door between waking and visionary rest open.
skullcap.historicUses: Native American ceremonial nerve herb. 19th c. anti-convulsant. Modern: anxiety, insomnia, nervous tension; GABA modulation studies.
skullcap.compounds: Baicalin, scutellarein, wogonin. Anxiolytic via GABA. Antioxidant.

motherwort.desc: The lion-hearted mother herb. Calms the anxious heart and steadies the hand of those who must be brave.
motherwort.descFull: Leonurus cardiaca, the lion-hearted herb, steadies the trembling heart and eases the terror of anxiety. Long revered in women's rites, it is equally a herb of courage, calming what the mind has set racing in the chest.
motherwort.historicUses: European midwife herb for uterine toning. Chinese Yi Mu Cao for postpartum. Modern: cardiac palpitations; anxiolytic; uterine stimulant research.
motherwort.cautions: CONTRAINDICATED in pregnancy. May interact with anticoagulants.

angelica.desc: The archangel's root. A tall guardian against curse and plague, standing at the border of seen and unseen.
angelica.descFull: Angelica rises tall and crowned, its great root a ward against curse, plague, and malevolent spirits. Carried as a talisman in times of sickness, burned to clear the air of ill intent, held sacred in Nordic tradition.
angelica.historicUses: Nordic plague protector 15th c. Laplander sacred herb. Benedictine liqueur base. Modern: digestive bitter; carminative; anti-inflammatory; anti-fungal research.
angelica.cautions: Furanocoumarins (photosensitizing), terpenes, phthalides. Avoid in pregnancy and UV exposure.

black cohosh.props: Feminine Power · Change · Strength · Ancestral Work
black cohosh.desc: Root of the deep woods. Held by native women as a sovereign herb. Eases the fires of change with quiet authority.
black cohosh.descFull: Rising from the deep Appalachian woodland, black cohosh root has long been a sovereign herb of women's power. Eases the fires of transition with quiet authority, honored as a plant of ancestral lineage and bodily sovereignty.
black cohosh.historicUses: Algonquin and Cherokee women's herb for menstrual and menopausal pain. 19th c. Eclectic medicine. Modern: menopause symptoms; hormonal support.
black cohosh.pairings: Red clover · Shatavari · Wild yam · Motherwort
black cohosh.compounds: Triterpene glycosides (actein), isoflavones. Modulates estrogen pathways.
black cohosh.cautions: Rare hepatotoxicity. Avoid in hormone-sensitive cancers.

california poppy.desc: The golden field-sleeper. Softer than its opium cousin, it lulls the restless to gentle oblivion.
california poppy.descFull: Fields of golden california poppy pulse at the edge of sleep. Unlike its opium cousin it carries no narcotic risk, only a soft sedative grace that quiets the nerves and lulls the restless mind to gentle, dreaming oblivion.
california poppy.historicUses: Native Californian analgesic and sleep aid. Chumash and Costanoan traditional medicine. Modern: mild anxiolytic; sleep quality studies; non-addictive sedative.
california poppy.cautions: Mild CNS sedative. Avoid with pharmaceutical sedatives or MAOIs.

cat's claw.desc: The vine that grips like a claw and heals like a fortress. Amazonian warrior medicine, old and fierce.
cat's claw.descFull: From the Amazonian canopy, cat's claw vines grip the trees with curved thorns and gift their medicine freely. Amazonian peoples used it as a warrior tonic, building immunity against sickness and inflammation.
cat's claw.historicUses: Asháninka and Campa traditional immune tonic. Used for arthritis and inflammation. Modern: immune modulation; DNA repair studies; anti-tumor research.
cat's claw.compounds: Oxindole and pentacyclic alkaloids. Immunomodulatory. Anti-inflammatory.
cat's claw.cautions: Avoid in pregnancy and with immunosuppressants.

dong quai.props: Blood · Feminine Rites · Renewal · Moon Work
dong quai.desc: The female ginseng of Chinese medicine. Nourishes the blood, eases the cycle, honors the lunar body.
dong quai.descFull: Called the female ginseng of the East, dong quai nourishes and moves the blood, eases the discomfort of the monthly cycle, and honors the rhythms of the lunar body. A root of deep feminine renewal over two thousand years.
dong quai.historicUses: TCM blood tonic for 2000+ years. Menstrual regulation in classical Chinese texts. Modern: dysmenorrhea; anemia adjunct; menopausal support.
dong quai.pairings: Shatavari · Red raspberry leaf · Black cohosh · Ginger
dong quai.cautions: Mild estrogenic. Anticoagulant; avoid with warfarin. Photosensitizing.

eleuthero.desc: Siberian root of endurance. Taken by soldiers and explorers, it bends without breaking in storm or cold.
eleuthero.descFull: Known as Siberian ginseng, eleuthero is the root of endurance. Soviet athletes and cosmonauts relied on it. It steadies the stress response, supports stamina, and makes the body supple in the face of hardship.
eleuthero.historicUses: Traditional Chinese tonic for chronic disease. Soviet elite-athlete and cosmonaut adaptogen. Modern: adaptogen; immune support; mental performance under stress.
eleuthero.compounds: Eleutherosides (lignan glycosides, syringin). HPA axis modulation. Immune enhancer.
eleuthero.cautions: May interact with anticoagulants and digoxin.

goldenrod.desc: Gold wands of the late-summer meadow. Where goldenrod grows, the purse is said never to be truly empty.
goldenrod.descFull: Goldenrod blazes like captured sun across the late-summer meadow. Where it grows, folk wisdom holds that fortune favors the keeper of the land. A divination herb, kidney cleanser, and ward against bitterness of spirit.
goldenrod.historicUses: Native American wound herb and tea. European diuretic folk medicine. Modern: urinary tract support; quercetin research; anti-inflammatory.
goldenrod.compounds: Quercetin, rutin, saponins, terpenes. Anti-inflammatory. Diuretic. Antispasmodic.
goldenrod.cautions: Avoid in kidney disease. Ragweed-allergy risk.

hawthorn.desc: The faery thorn. Sacred and feared, it guards the heart in body and spirit alike. Never cut alone.
hawthorn.descFull: The hawthorn is the faery thorn of Celtic lore, standing at portal and crossroads, never cut without caution. Its berries strengthen the aging heart, its blossoms open gateways. Sacred at Beltane; planted at the edge of the known world.
hawthorn.historicUses: Celtic faery and Beltane threshold tree. Medieval heart tonic. Modern: cardiovascular support; blood pressure; CHF adjunct in clinical trials.
hawthorn.compounds: Oligomeric proanthocyanidins, vitexin, hyperoside. Cardiotonic. Vasodilatory.

kava.desc: The ceremonial root of Oceania. Stills conflict, opens fellowship, and parts the veil with ceremony.
kava.descFull: Kava root has been stirred into ceremony across Oceania for three thousand years, stilling conflict and binding community in shared ritual. Its gentle numbing warmth eases anxiety, opens the spirit, and parts the veil with calm.
kava.historicUses: Polynesian and Melanesian ceremonial drink. Peace-making and greeting rite. Modern: anxiety and insomnia treatment; GABAergic mechanism; WHO safety review.
kava.cautions: Hepatotoxic with heavy use or alcohol. Avoid in liver disease.

licorice root.desc: Sweeter than honey, older than Rome. Soothes the wounded throat and eases the ache of troubled passage.
licorice root.descFull: Glycyrrhiza, sweetness of the root. Licorice has softened the sore throat, eased the gut, and governed the adrenal response since Egyptian antiquity. Found in the tomb of Tutankhamun; prized across every civilization that encountered it.
licorice root.historicUses: Egyptian mummification and medicinal drink. Traditional Chinese adaptogenic tonic. Modern: adrenal support; ulcer treatment; expectorant.
licorice root.compounds: Glycyrrhizin (anti-inflammatory, antiviral), flavonoids, saponins. Adrenal tonic. Expectorant.

meadowsweet.desc: The bride of the meadow. Most sacred herb of the Druids, strewn at weddings and floated on sacred pools.
meadowsweet.descFull: Meadowsweet was among the three most sacred herbs of the Druids. Strewn at weddings to bless unions, floated on sacred pools as offering, its white flowers scent the summer air with honey and almonds. Aspirin was first derived here.
meadowsweet.historicUses: Druidic sacred strewing herb. Medieval mead-flavoring. Aspirin compound first isolated 1839. Modern: anti-inflammatory; gastroprotective.
meadowsweet.compounds: Salicylates, flavonoids, tannins. Anti-inflammatory. Analgesic. Gastroprotective.

oat straw.desc: The humble straw of the hearth harvest. Feeds the frayed nerve as grain feeds the winter-hollow body.
oat straw.descFull: From the grain fields of the Celtic heartland, oat straw is the sustaining herb of the nervous system. Where other herbs startle, oat straw simply nourishes, rebuilding the exhausted nerve over weeks of faithful use.
oat straw.historicUses: European staple crop medicine for nervous exhaustion. Eclectic herbalism nervine tonic. Modern: anxiety; cognitive support; demulcent; nervous system nourishment.
oat straw.compounds: Avenanthramides, beta-glucan, silicic acid, B vitamins. Nervine tonic. Anti-inflammatory.

red clover.desc: The trifoliate charm of meadow and myth. Find four leaves and see the faeries; keep the three and hold fortune.
red clover.descFull: Red clover is the trifoliate charm of meadow and Celtic myth. Four leaves grant faery sight; three leaves bring fortune and protection. Its blossoms have long been steeped for lung health and women's wellbeing.
red clover.historicUses: Celtic luck and faery charm. European respiratory folk tonic. Modern: isoflavone source for menopausal symptoms; bone density; cardiovascular research.
red clover.compounds: Isoflavones (formononetin, biochanin A), flavonoids. Phytoestrogen. Mild anticoagulant.

red raspberry leaf.desc: The leaf of the briar-mother. Tones the womb, strengthens the blood, and prepares the body for great passage.
red raspberry leaf.descFull: The leaf behind the berry, red raspberry leaf tones the uterine muscle and strengthens the blood. Midwives throughout Europe prescribed it for preparation and recovery. A nourishing brew for cycles of change and birth.
red raspberry leaf.historicUses: European midwife tonic for pregnancy. First Nations women's tea. Modern: uterine toning; iron and magnesium source; menstrual cramping; postpartum recovery.
red raspberry leaf.pairings: Nettle · Oat straw · Dong quai · Lemon
red raspberry leaf.compounds: Fragarine (uterine tonic), tannins, flavonoids, iron, magnesium. Astringent.

reishi.desc: The mushroom of immortality. Grows where old trees breathe their last and gives that breath back to the living.
reishi.descFull: Known in China as the mushroom of immortality, reishi grows on dying trees and returns vitality to the living. Emperors sought it for centuries. Taken over long seasons, it steadies the immune response and calms the spirit.
reishi.historicUses: Imperial Chinese longevity medicine 2000+ years. Taoist immortality herb. Modern: immunomodulation; anti-tumor research; liver protection; adaptogenic.
reishi.compounds: Beta-glucans, triterpenes (ganoderic acids), polysaccharides. Immunomodulator. May thin blood; stop before surgery.

rhodiola.props: Endurance · Mental Clarity · Courage · High Places
rhodiola.desc: Root of the Arctic ridgeline. Viking explorers carried it for strength at the edge of the world.
rhodiola.descFull: Rhodiola clings to Arctic and Alpine ridges where little else survives. Norse peoples carried it for endurance on long voyages. It sharpens the mind under pressure, steadies the heart under stress, and lifts the spirit from darkness.
rhodiola.historicUses: Norse and Viking endurance herb. Siberian folk adaptogen. Modern: mental performance under stress; antidepressant-adjacent; fatigue; cortisol modulation.
rhodiola.cautions: MAO-inhibiting at high doses. Avoid in bipolar or with SSRI/MAOI.

schisandra.desc: The berry of five flavors, touching every sense. A balancing tonic for the liver, mind, and the tired spirit.
schisandra.descFull: Schisandra berries carry all five flavors, touching all five organ systems in Chinese medicine. A supreme adaptogen and liver tonic, it sharpens the mind and refills the well of exhaustion with patient, steady force.
schisandra.historicUses: TCM Qi tonic for 2000+ years. Russian adaptogen research 1950s-70s. Modern: liver protection; stress adaptation; cognitive performance; athletic endurance.
schisandra.cautions: Hepatoprotective. May interact with cytochrome P450 drugs.

shatavari.props: Feminine Wisdom · Nourishment · Love · Lunar Depth
shatavari.desc: She who has a thousand husbands. Ayurveda's queen of herbs, nourishing the feminine essence through all seasons.
shatavari.descFull: Shatavari, she who has a thousand husbands, is the Ayurvedic queen of feminine nourishment. Its cooling roots feed the reproductive tissues, balance the hormonal tides, and sustain through every transition from maiden to elder.
shatavari.historicUses: Ayurvedic Rasayana tonic for 2000+ years. Galactagogue in South Asian midwifery. Modern: hormonal balance; fertility; adaptogen; gastric ulcer protection.
shatavari.compounds: Saponins (shatavarin I-V), flavonoids, polysaccharides. Phytoestrogenic. Adaptogenic. Galactagogue.

slippery elm.desc: The whispering bark. Its inner wood dissolves to a silken gel that heals every inflamed passage it touches.
slippery elm.descFull: The inner bark of slippery elm dissolves to a silken, nourishing gel that soothes every inflamed passage it enters. Used by Iroquois healers and frontier medicine alike. Its branches were said to carry messages between worlds.
slippery elm.historicUses: Iroquois poultice and internal tonic. American frontier survival food and wound dressing. Modern: IBS; GERD; IBD adjunct; throat demulcent.
slippery elm.pairings: Marshmallow root · Ginger · Licorice root · Chamomile
slippery elm.compounds: Mucilaginous polysaccharides, tannins. Demulcent. Anti-inflammatory. Prebiotic. Coats and soothes GI mucosa.
slippery elm.cautions: May slow absorption of concurrent medications; take separately. Avoid in elm allergy. Safety in pregnancy not established.

white willow bark.props: Moon · Grief · Healing Pain · Dream Sight
white willow bark.desc: The moon's own tree. Ancient source of aspirin's ancestor. Eases pain as the moon eases the pull of the tide.
white willow bark.descFull: The weeping willow bends over sacred waters, the moon's own tree in Celtic tradition. Its bark holds salicin, the ancestor of aspirin, and has eased pain and fever since ancient Egypt. The tree of grief, healing, and the night-dreaming mind.
white willow bark.historicUses: Egyptian and Greek pain and fever bark. Celtic lunar sacred tree. Aspirin precursor isolated 1828. Modern: anti-inflammatory; analgesic; fever reduction.
white willow bark.compounds: Salicin (converts to salicylic acid), tannins, flavonoids. Anti-inflammatory. Analgesic.
white willow bark.cautions: Avoid in aspirin allergy or anticoagulants.

witch hazel.desc: The late-blooming water-witch. Its forked branches found the spring; its bark closes the bleeding wound.
witch hazel.descFull: Witch hazel blooms while others have gone bare, its yellow ribbons threading through November frost. Its forked branches served as dowsing rods; its bark is a powerful astringent from Mohegan healing tradition that closes wounds.
witch hazel.historicUses: Mohegan and Oneida wound herb and decoction. American patent medicine for skin care. Modern: topical astringent; hemorrhoid treatment; anti-inflammatory.
witch hazel.compounds: Hamamelitannins, proanthocyanidins, gallic acid. Astringent. Anti-inflammatory. Antioxidant. Topical use only.
witch hazel.cautions: Internal use causes GI irritation and possible liver stress at high doses. External application only. Avoid near eyes and mucous membranes.

yellow dock.desc: Root that remembers the iron of the earth. Feeds the blood and clears the liver of what has grown stale.
yellow dock.descFull: Yellow dock drives its taproot deep into iron-rich earth and returns that iron to the blood. A powerful spring tonic of Celtic hedgerow medicine, it clears the liver, feeds the blood, and moves what has grown sluggish through winter.
yellow dock.historicUses: Celtic spring cleansing tonic. European blood-builder for anemia. Modern: iron-bioavailable supplement; liver stimulant; skin conditions; chronic inflammation.
yellow dock.compounds: Anthraquinones (laxative), oxalates, tannins. Hepatic tonic. Mild laxative.
yellow dock.cautions: Avoid in kidney disease. Cathartic in excess.

benzoin.desc: The sweet resin of consecration. Burned to sanctify sacred space and draw good fortune from the ether.
benzoin.descFull: Benzoin resin drips from the wounded bark of the styrax tree in golden tears. Burned in temples and apothecary workrooms alike, its sweet vanilla-balsam smoke purifies sacred space, draws prosperity, and seals ritual work.
benzoin.historicUses: Incense in Hindu, Buddhist, and Christian rites. Medieval apothecary preservative (Friar's Balsam). Modern: wound sealant tincture; mucolytic expectorant; antiseptic.
benzoin.compounds: Benzoic acid, benzyl benzoate, vanillin. Antiseptic. Expectorant.
benzoin.cautions: Skin sensitizer in some; avoid undiluted contact.

dragon's blood.desc: Crimson resin of the dragon-tree. Burned to amplify intention, ward the circle, and banish what will not leave.
dragon's blood.descFull: Dragon's blood drips from the Dracaena tree in drops that dry to crimson flakes, the color of war and power. Burned in ritual to amplify intention, seal the circle against intrusion, and banish what refuses gentler persuasion.
dragon's blood.historicUses: Ancient Roman and Greek wound treatment. Medieval magical amplifier. Modern: antifungal research; antimicrobial; wound healing; anti-inflammatory compounds.
dragon's blood.compounds: Dracorhodin (red pigment), flavonoids, terpenes. Antimicrobial. Anti-inflammatory. Antifungal.
dragon's blood.cautions: Limited human data; caution in pregnancy.

mastic.desc: Crystalline tears of the Mediterranean mastic tree. Chewed since antiquity for clarity, breath, and holy smoke.
mastic.descFull: Mastic crystals form as tears on the Pistacia lentiscus of Chios. Chewed for fresh breath since antiquity, burned as incense in Orthodox churches, and studied for gastric and antimicrobial action in modern laboratories.
mastic.historicUses: Ancient Greek and Roman digestive and breath freshener. Byzantine incense resin. Modern: H. pylori treatment; gastric ulcer; antifungal; dental antibacterial.
mastic.compounds: Alpha-pinene, beta-myrcene, masticadienonic acid. Antibacterial (H. pylori). Anti-inflammatory. Antifungal.

oak bark.props: Strength · Endurance · Sacred Power · Druidic Core
oak bark.desc: The Druids' most sacred tree. Oak bark holds the old strength. Nothing that matters was built without the oak.
oak bark.descFull: The oak is the Druids' axis mundi, their most sacred tree. Its bark holds deep tannins that tighten and protect every tissue it touches. Nothing that endures was made without oak: ships, halls, wheels, and rites of passage.
oak bark.historicUses: Druidic central sacred tree and ritual site. Celtic oath-tree. Medieval tanning and wound astringent. Modern: venous insufficiency; diarrhea; anti-inflammatory.
oak bark.compounds: Quercitannic acid, ellagitannins, quercetin. Astringent. Anti-inflammatory. Antiviral.
oak bark.cautions: High tannin; limit extended internal use.

pine resin.desc: Amber tears of the evergreen. Burns to purify the hall and steady the lung through the cold-season nights.
pine resin.descFull: The pine bleeds amber resin in the cold forest, an offering of the evergreen that endures all winter. Burned to clear the air and bless the hall, inhaled to open the chest and lungs, carried as a protective talisman.
pine resin.historicUses: Norse midwinter hearth burn. Ancient Greek respiratory treatment. Modern: inhalation decongestant; antimicrobial surface properties; aromatherapy.
pine resin.compounds: Alpha-pinene, beta-pinene, limonene. Expectorant via inhalation. Antimicrobial. Decongestant.

sandalwood.desc: The heartwood of stillness. Its scent clears the mind and opens the door between the outer world and the within.
sandalwood.descFull: Sandalwood heartwood holds its scent for decades, patient and still. Burned in temples from India to Rome, it clears the chattering mind and opens the bridge between this world and the inner one. A supreme meditation companion.
sandalwood.historicUses: Vedic ritual incense 4000+ years. Ayurvedic skin and fever medicine. Buddhist altar offering. Modern: anxiolytic aromatherapy; antibacterial; anti-inflammatory.
sandalwood.compounds: Alpha-santalol, beta-santalol (sesquiterpenes). Anxiolytic. Antibacterial. Anti-inflammatory. Grounding aromatic.
sandalwood.cautions: Source ethically; wild stocks are declining. Dilute essential oil before skin application. Avoid undiluted topical use.

borage.props: Courage · Joy · Psychic Power · Warrior Heart
borage.desc: The herb of gladness. Celtic warriors steeped it in wine before battle to banish fear and call courage.
borage.descFull: Borage, the herb of gladness and courage. Celtic warriors steeped it in wine before battle; Pliny wrote that it made men merry. Its star-shaped blue flowers are a symbol of courage; its seed oil is among the richest plant sources of GLA.
borage.historicUses: Celtic and Roman pre-battle courage herb. Medieval cordial flower. Modern: GLA-rich seed oil for skin conditions; adrenal support; anti-inflammatory.
borage.compounds: Seed oil safe. Anti-inflammatory via GLA.
borage.cautions: GLA (gamma-linolenic acid) in seed oil; pyrrolizidine alkaloids in leaf (hepatotoxic).

jasmine.props: Love · Moon · Prophetic Dreams · Spiritual Opening
jasmine.desc: Moon-scented and intoxicating. Jasmine opens the heart to love and the sleeping mind to prophecy.
jasmine.descFull: Jasmine blooms at night, a moon-herb of intoxicating fragrance that opens the heart to love and the sleeping mind to prophetic vision. Worn in the hair at sacred festivals; strewn on the marriage bed; burned at dream-working threshold.
jasmine.historicUses: Sufi and Mughal ceremonial flower. Chinese anxiety and liver herb in TCM. Modern: aromatherapy for anxiety; oxytocin studies; mild antispasmodic.
jasmine.compounds: Benzyl acetate, linalool, indole. Anxiolytic via olfaction. Mild antispasmodic.
jasmine.cautions: Concentrated extract sensitizes skin; patch test.

linden flower.desc: The faery linden tree. Sweetly scented blossoms calm the fevered brow and invite the gentlest of dreams.
linden flower.descFull: The linden tree is the faery tree of central European lore. Celtic and Germanic peoples gathered beneath it for justice, love pledges, and the sweet summer nights of Midsummer. Its blossoms calm fever and invite gentle dreams.
linden flower.historicUses: Germanic faery and justice tree. French tilleul nervine tea. Modern: cold and flu; nervous tension; mild sedative; antispasmodic.
linden flower.compounds: Flavonoids (quercetin, kaempferol), mucilage, volatile oils. Diaphoretic. Mild sedative. Antispasmodic.

violet.desc: The gentle flower of the forgotten. Heals old grief, draws love, and marks the threshold of the faery mound.
violet.descFull: Violet grows in the shadow of the old trees, a modest faery flower that marks the hidden thresholds. It heals old grief with patient sweetness, draws love to the tender-hearted, and its leaves ease the throat and soothe the chest.
violet.historicUses: Athenian symbol and grief rite flower. Celtic grave and faery flower. Modern: demulcent for coughs; vitamin C leaf; anti-inflammatory.
violet.compounds: Rutin, vitamin C, salicylic acid, mucilage, anthocyanins. Anti-inflammatory. Antioxidant. Demulcent. Expectorant.
violet.cautions: Roots and seeds emetic in large quantities; use flowers and leaves only. Avoid therapeutic doses in aspirin sensitivity (salicylates present).

chaga.desc: The birch canker of the north. Grows for decades in silence, storing the forest's immune memory in black mass.
chaga.descFull: Chaga grows on birch trees in the boreal north, a dark woody mass that takes twenty years to reach medicinal size. Siberian and Finnish folk healers brewed it for centuries, drawing on its dense polysaccharide immune memory.
chaga.historicUses: Siberian Khanty and Komi folk cancer tea. Finnish immunity tonic. Modern: antioxidant; immune modulation; tumor research (beta-glucans); adaptogenic.
chaga.compounds: Beta-glucans, betulinic acid, melanin, triterpenoids. Potent antioxidant. Immunomodulatory. Anticoagulant.

lion's mane.props: Mind · Clarity · Nerve Healing · Creative Fire
lion's mane.desc: White cascading mushroom of the awakened mind. Rebuilds the nerve and opens the gates of clear thought.
lion's mane.descFull: Lion's mane cascades in white tendrils from ancient oaks. Consumed by Chinese monks for centuries to sharpen concentration. Modern research confirms its nerve growth factor stimulation, a rare medicinal gift in the fungal world.
lion's mane.historicUses: Chinese Buddhist monastery cognitive herb. Traditional tonic for stomach and nerve. Modern: NGF stimulation; Alzheimer's research; neuropathy; anxiety and depression.
lion's mane.compounds: Hericenones, erinacines (NGF stimulators), beta-glucans, ergosterol. Neuroprotective. Cognitive support. Immune-modulating.
lion's mane.cautions: Rare GI upset at first use. Avoid in mushroom allergy. May potentiate anticoagulants. Safety in pregnancy not established.

turkey tail.desc: The banded shelf mushroom of every forest floor. Builds immunity the way the forest builds itself: slowly, together.
turkey tail.descFull: Turkey tail fans across fallen logs in bands of rust and cream, nature's mandala. Among the most studied medicinal mushrooms, its polysaccharides train the immune system with patient, forest-slow persistence.
turkey tail.historicUses: Chinese TCM lung and immune herb. Japanese PSK cancer adjunct from 1970s. Modern: adjunct cancer immunotherapy; gut microbiome support; antiviral.
turkey tail.compounds: PSK and PSP polysaccharides, beta-glucans, ergosterol, flavonoids. Immunomodulatory. Anti-tumor adjunct. Antioxidant.
turkey tail.cautions: Generally well tolerated. Rare GI upset. Avoid in mushroom allergy. May enhance immunosuppressant drug effects; consult if on biologics.

cordyceps.props: Vitality · Endurance · Lung Power · Hidden Strength
cordyceps.desc: Born of the high plateau between insect and fungus. Fills the lung with altitude's endurance and fires the blood.
cordyceps.descFull: Cordyceps grows at extreme altitude on the Tibetan Plateau. Tibetan herders noted their yaks grew strong eating it. It fills the lung, oxygenates the blood, and grants the endurance of the mountain to those who dwell below.
cordyceps.historicUses: Tibetan high-altitude endurance herb for centuries. TCM Qi and Yang tonic. Modern: athletic performance; lung function; anti-aging; immune modulation; libido research.
cordyceps.compounds: Cordycepin (adenosine analog), beta-glucans. Improves oxygen utilization. Immunomodulatory. Adaptogenic.

amla.desc: Sacred gooseberry of the Vedas. Highest natural vitamin C source. Ayurveda's fruit of immortality.
amla.descFull: Amla, the Indian gooseberry, is among the most revered plants in Ayurvedic medicine and a component of Triphala. Its vitamin C resists cooking and oxidation. Sacred to Vishnu; offered at funerals and festivals alike.
amla.historicUses: Ayurvedic Rasayana longevity fruit for 3000+ years. Component of Triphala formula. Modern: antioxidant; vitamin C supplement; liver protection; anti-diabetic research.
amla.compounds: Emblicanin A/B, ellagitannins, heat-stable ascorbic acid. Potent antioxidant.
amla.cautions: Hepatoprotective. Hypoglycemic; caution with diabetes meds.

brahmi.desc: The scholar's herb of India's still water margins. Clears the mind for learning and settles the anxious thought.
brahmi.descFull: Brahmi grows along the margins of still water, patient and low, and it asks the mind to follow its nature. Used by Vedic scholars to memorize long sacred texts, it improves memory consolidation and gently quiets the anxiety that blocks learning.
brahmi.historicUses: Ayurvedic scholar's memory tonic for 3000 years. Vedic sacred herb. Modern: memory and cognitive function; anxiety; ADHD adjunct; neuroprotection.
brahmi.compounds: Bacosides A and B (triterpenoid saponins), alkaloids. Memory encoding enhancer. Anxiolytic. Neuroprotective.
brahmi.cautions: Fat-soluble; take with food for absorption. May slow heart rate. Avoid in bradycardia. Safety in pregnancy not established.

moringa.desc: The miracle tree of the tropics. Every part of it feeds and heals, from leaf to seed to root and bark.
moringa.descFull: Moringa is called the miracle tree across tropical Africa and Asia, and the name is earned. Its leaves outstrip most vegetables in protein and iron. Its pods clarify water. Its bark soothes inflammation. A complete act of generosity in tree form.
moringa.historicUses: Traditional medicine across Africa, India, Philippines for malnutrition. Ancient Mauryan empire food crop. Modern: micronutrient supplement; anti-inflammatory; blood sugar.
moringa.compounds: Isothiocyanates, quercetin, beta-carotene, iron, calcium, amino acids. Anti-inflammatory. Hypoglycemic.

neem.desc: The bitter healer of India's roadsides. Purifies the blood, drives off pests, and speaks in the language of truth.
neem.descFull: Neem is the pharmacy and guardian of the Indian village, its every part bearing bitter medicine. Its bitterness itself is a teaching: that which protects does not always comfort. Used for skin, blood, and as a deterrent against blight and pests.
neem.historicUses: Ayurvedic purifier and pest deterrent for 4000+ years. Sacred to Shiva. Modern: antimicrobial; antifungal; anti-parasitic; anti-inflammatory; blood glucose modulation.
neem.compounds: Azadirachtin, nimbidin, nimbolide, limonoids. Antibacterial. Antifungal. Blood glucose lowering.
neem.cautions: Toxic in high doses; unsafe for infants.

black seed.desc: A cure for all but death, the Prophet said. Ancient black seed oil from Egypt's temples. Fierce and gentle at once.
black seed.descFull: Found in Tutankhamun's tomb; praised in Islamic tradition as a cure for all but death. Black seed has been used across Egypt, Arabia, and South Asia for three millennia, for the chest, the skin, the blood, and the blessing of the body.
black seed.historicUses: Egyptian tomb medicine. Islamic Tibb al-Nabawi medicine. Ancient Greek tonic. Modern: anti-inflammatory; immune modulation; anti-diabetic; blood pressure; anti-tumor.
black seed.cautions: May lower blood pressure; avoid high doses pregnant.

myrtle.desc: Sacred to Venus and the bridal bower. Myrtle was worn at weddings from Athens to Rome to Celtic coastal shores.
myrtle.descFull: Myrtle adorned every bridal wreath from Athens to Rome, sacred to Venus and the promise of lasting love. Its white flowers and glossy leaves speak of beauty, fidelity, and the immortal aspiration of the heart.
myrtle.historicUses: Greek and Roman bridal and Venus herb. British royal wedding bouquet inclusion (ongoing tradition). Modern: antibacterial; antioxidant; respiratory tonic.
myrtle.compounds: Myrtucommulone, 1,8-cineole, anthocyanins. Antibacterial. Antioxidant. Expectorant. Anti-inflammatory.

sea buckthorn.desc: The golden berry of the sea cliffs. Sustains the traveler through cold and deprivation; lights the body from within.
sea buckthorn.descFull: Sea buckthorn clings to windswept sea cliffs in blazing orange clusters, a survival berry carried by Mongol warriors and Himalayan mountaineers. Its concentrated nutrition lights the body from within and heals the skin in the coldest passage.
sea buckthorn.historicUses: Tibetan and Mongolian folk medicine for energy and wound healing. Himalayan mountaineer survival berry. Modern: skin healing; cardiovascular; vitamin C and E; omega-7.
sea buckthorn.compounds: Omega-7 (palmitoleic acid), vitamins C, E, A, flavonoids, carotenoids. Skin-regenerating. Antioxidant.

blue lotus.props: Vision · Ecstasy · Lunar Mystery · Sacred Opening
blue lotus.desc: The dream-flower of the Nile. Held at banquets and painted in tombs. Opens the inner eye with gentle intoxication.
blue lotus.descFull: Blue lotus floats above sacred water in radiant blue, a flower the Egyptians revered for mildly visionary properties. Held at banquets, depicted in tomb paintings, and offered to the gods. The flower between sleep and knowing.
blue lotus.historicUses: Ancient Egyptian sacred visionary and ceremonial flower. Depicted in 3000+ year tomb art. Modern: mild sedative; anxiolytic; aporphine alkaloid study.
blue lotus.compounds: Aporphine, nuciferine (dopamine modulators), antioxidant flavonoids. Mild psychoactive. Anxiolytic.
blue lotus.cautions: Avoid in pregnancy and with MAOIs.

wood betony.desc: The head-clearing herb of the Anglo-Saxon leech. Grounds the spirit and wards the wandering mind against attack.
wood betony.descFull: Wood betony was among the most important herbs in the Anglo-Saxon Lacnunga herbal, recommended for a multitude of ills. It grounds the scattered spirit, eases the aching head, and wards the mind against what seeks to enter uninvited.
wood betony.historicUses: Anglo-Saxon primary healing herb (Lacnunga MS). Roman legionary wound herb. Medieval 29-ailment cure-all. Modern: headache; nervous anxiety; digestive bitters.
wood betony.compounds: Betaine, tannins, alkaloids, diterpenoids. Bitter tonic. Nervine. Mild antihypertensive.

agrimony.desc: The gentle warrior herb of the hedgerow. Reverses curses, heals wounds, and places the anxious to sleep.
agrimony.descFull: Agrimony stands quietly in the hedgerow, burred seed heads catching the wool of passing sheep. In folk magic it reverses curses back to their sender. Its bitter tannins close the wound and soothe the troubled sleep of those who lay awake in worry.
agrimony.historicUses: Anglo-Saxon Lacnunga wound herb. Scottish folk curse-reversal plant. Medieval liver tonic. Modern: sore throat; diarrhea; urinary tract; mild astringent.
agrimony.pairings: Oak bark · Yarrow · Calendula · Witch hazel
agrimony.compounds: Agrimophol, tannins, flavonoids, bitter glycosides. Astringent. Anti-inflammatory. Cholagogue.

boneset.props: Healing · Fever Breaking · Bone-Deep Work · Purification
boneset.desc: The deep-ache breaker. Native American fever herb for the bone-crushing illness they called break-bone fever.
boneset.descFull: Boneset earned its name from Native American treatment of influenza so severe it was called break-bone fever for the deep aching pain it caused. The herb eases that depth of sickness, breaks the fever, and clears the respiratory passages.
boneset.historicUses: Iroquois and Algonquin primary fever and flu medicine. Civil War battlefield herb. Modern: immune modulation; anti-inflammatory; upper respiratory; febrifuge.
boneset.compounds: Sesquiterpene lactones (eupatoriopicrin), flavonoids, polysaccharides. Immunostimulant. Diaphoretic. Anti-inflammatory.

uva ursi.props: Purification · Psychic Power · Sacred Smoke · Healing
uva ursi.desc: Bear's grape of the high moorland. A urinary purifier smoked in Native sacred rites and steeped by healers.
uva ursi.descFull: Bearberry trails across moorland and boreal heath. Native peoples blended it in kinnikinnick smoking mixtures for sacred ceremony. Celtic healers steeped its astringent leaves for kidney and bladder ailments, a plant of the high spare places.
uva ursi.historicUses: Native American kinnikinnick ceremonial smoke. European urinary tract folk medicine. Modern: UTI treatment (arbutin); antiseptic urinary; astringent.
uva ursi.compounds: Arbutin (urinary hydroquinone prodrug), tannins, ursolic acid, allantoin. Urinary antiseptic. Astringent.
uva ursi.cautions: Short-term use only (7-10 days); extended use is hepato- and nephrotoxic. Contraindicated in pregnancy, kidney disease, and children under 12.

wild yam.props: Balance · Feminine Strength · Antispasm · Moon Root
wild yam.desc: The root beneath the fertile soil. Eases the cramping grip and steadies the hormonal tide of the moon-body.
wild yam.descFull: Wild yam twines through the woodland understory, its tubers holding diosgenin, the compound from which synthetic progesterone was first derived. Long used by Native midwives for cramping and labor support, a root of the feminine deep.
wild yam.historicUses: Native American anti-spasmodic and birthing herb. Eclectic medicine for menstrual cramps. Modern: diosgenin source for steroid synthesis; antispasmodic; anti-inflammatory.
wild yam.pairings: Black cohosh · Dong quai · Shatavari · Motherwort
wild yam.compounds: Diosgenin (steroidal saponin), alkaloids, tannins. Antispasmodic. Anti-inflammatory. Source of pharmaceutical steroid synthesis.
wild yam.cautions: Body cannot convert diosgenin to progesterone directly; do not substitute for hormone therapy. Avoid in estrogen-sensitive conditions.

mustard seed.desc: The parable-seed. Small as doubt, powerful as certainty. Burns through cold, blockage, and complacency alike.
mustard seed.descFull: Mustard seed appears in every wisdom tradition as the emblem of great power from small beginning. Its ground seed draws blood to the surface and breaks congestion. Burned to purify space and drive off spirits that feed on stagnation.
mustard seed.historicUses: Roman and Greek warming poultice. Biblical wisdom parable. Medieval plague-house fumigant. Modern: circulatory stimulant; mustard plaster for chest congestion.
mustard seed.compounds: Allyl isothiocyanate, glucosinolates, selenium, omega-3. Rubefacient. Expectorant. Antimicrobial.
mustard seed.cautions: Avoid on broken or sensitive skin.
